### Project

cortex

### Description

In the **AI Agents** panel, hovering over items in the **Filter Actions** dropdown/list does not show any tooltip/help text. This makes it harder to understand each filter option quickly (especially for less obvious categories or truncated labels) and reduces accessibility.


### Error Message

```shell

```

### Debug Logs

```shell

```

```shell

As a result, follow-up logic can receive an invalid or meaningless path. ### Error Message ```shell ``` ### Debug Logs ```shell ``` ### System Information ```shell OS: Windows 11 Pro (10.0.26200, 64-bit) CPU: 11th Gen Intel Core i7-11700 @ 2.50GHz RAM: 32 GB total ``` ### Screenshots ### Steps to Reproduce 1. Render the panel with a whitespace-only path, e.g. `` (or a string of tabs). 2. Confirm that TimelineView is rendered instead of the empty-state message. ### Expected Behavior The component should treat a whitespace-only path as “no file”: trim or normalize before the conditional and show the empty state (“Select a file to view its timeline”) in that case. ### Actual Behavior A whitespace-only `filePath` is treated as valid; the empty-state branch is skipped and TimelineView is shown with an invalid path. ### Additional Context - Source: `src/components/timeline/TimelinePanel.tsx`, conditional `Show when={props.filePath}`. - This behavior is distinct from existing bounty issues on TimelinePanel (#22156 unreachable panel, #21749 handleCompare).

items.title: 📺 Bounty: Roku/Fire TV App for BoTTube (125 RTC)
items.body:
## Summary
Build a BoTTube TV client targeting Roku and Fire TV so users can browse and watch BoTTube videos from a television-focused interface.

## Why This Matters
This bounty is about opening a new viewing surface for BoTTube, not just shipping a mock TV shell. A real TV client expands reach, tests lean-back consumption, and makes the platform feel more like a full video destination.

## Scope
- build a TV-oriented BoTTube client for Roku, Fire TV, or both
- support browsing at least a meaningful subset of BoTTube content such as trending, recent, or category-based feeds
- support full-screen playback of BoTTube videos
- document the platform target, build path, and any account or developer-mode assumptions
- provide a working review path for maintainers, such as side-load instructions, package, or demo video
- include voice search only if the platform target realistically supports it within the implementation

## Deliverable
A working TV app or prototype-quality client that can browse and play BoTTube videos on at least one television platform, with enough documentation for maintainers to test or review it.

## Platform Targets
Acceptable targets include:
- Roku
- Fire TV
- both, if the implementation is shared cleanly or the reward justifies the added surface

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] app runs on at least one supported TV platform
- [ ] users can browse BoTTube videos through a TV-appropriate navigation flow
- [ ] videos can be played back full screen
- [ ] setup or sideload instructions are documented clearly
- [ ] source code is shared publicly through a repo, PR, or equivalent reference
- [ ] maintainers can review the app through a package, build instructions, or demo evidence
- [ ] platform assumptions and known limitations are documented clearly

## Verification Proof
Post proof in this issue including:
- target platform implemented
- repo, PR, or source link
- build or sideload instructions
- screenshots or short demo video of browsing and playback
- notes on any platform limitations or unsupported features

## Payout
`125 RTC`

## Non-Goals
- a mock UI with no real video playback
- vague “TV support” planning without a runnable client
- unrelated mobile or web UI work
- forcing both Roku and Fire TV if only one working target can be delivered well
- undocumented platform-specific hacks that maintainers cannot review

## Claim Routing
Use this issue for TV app implementation proof and payout discussion. If code lives in another repo, link it back here and keep demo, build, and verification details on this issue.

## Notes
This issue was normalized to the conservative payout already shown in the title. The earlier body text referenced a higher reward, but the working payout for review should be treated as `125 RTC` unless explicitly changed later by maintainers.

items.title: [BOUNTY: 3 RTC] Add Your Machine to the Green Tracker — Machines Preserved from E-Waste
items.body:
## Bounty: 3 RTC

### The Green Tracker

Elyan Labs just launched the **Machines Preserved** tracker: [rustchain.org/preserved.html](https://rustchain.org/preserved.html)

It tracks every machine in our fleet that would otherwise be e-waste — PowerPC G4s from 2003, a 386 laptop from 1990, an IBM POWER8 mainframe, a Mac Pro trashcan. All still computing. All earning their keep.

**The punchline**: Our entire 16-machine fleet draws roughly the same power as ONE modern GPU mining rig (~2,000W) — while preventing 1,300+ kg of manufacturing CO2 and 250 kg of e-waste.

### What You Can Do

**Add your machine to the tracker!** If you're running a RustChain miner or have vintage/repurposed hardware that's still computing, we want to feature it.

### Requirements

1. Open a PR on [Scottcjn/Rustchain](https://github.com/Scottcjn/Rustchain) adding your machine to the green tracker data
2. Include:
   - Machine name/model
   - Year manufactured
   - Architecture (PowerPC G4, x86_64, ARM, Apple Silicon M1/M2/M4, SPARC, etc.)
   - Power draw (watts, approximate is fine)
   - What it's doing (mining, inference, dev, preserved)
   - Your miner ID or wallet name
3. Bonus: include a photo of the machine

### Examples of machines we want to see:
- That old MacBook you mine RTC on
- Your Raspberry Pi running a node
- A ThinkPad from 2008 that still works
- Your M2 MacBook Air earning 1.2x antiquity
- A Dell Optiplex you pulled from a dumpster
- Literally anything that's still computing instead of being in a landfill

### Why This Matters

The industry says old hardware is obsolete. We say **the greenest computation is the one that already exists**. A machine that's already built has zero manufacturing footprint — no new rare earth mining, no smelting, no shipping from Shenzhen.

Proof-of-Antiquity isn't nostalgia. It's a carbon incentive.

### Payout
- **3 RTC** per machine added with complete info
- **+2 RTC bonus** if you include a photo
- Multiple machines = multiple payouts (1 PR per machine please)

**Wallet:** Drop your wallet name when you submit.

---

*Every CPU deserves a voice. Yours does too.*

items.title: [BUG] [alpha] Rules Library switching a new rule from Custom to Project keeps the stale user file target
items.body:
### Project

ide

### Description

In the mounted **Rules Library** create flow, a newly created rule always starts with a user-file target path.

Visible result:

- open **Rules Library**,
- click **Create Rule**,
- change **Rule Type** from **Custom** to **Project**,
- fill in the rule and save it.

The save still uses the original user-file target instead of switching to a project rules file path.

So a rule the user explicitly changed to **Project** can still be created against `user/user.rules` rather than a project-scoped rules file.

### Root Cause

The mounted create action always starts a new rule as `user`:

- `/src/components/ai/RulesLibrary.tsx:113-119`
  - **Create Rule** calls `rulesLibrary.createNewRule()` with no source override
- `/src/context/RulesLibraryContext.tsx:765-779`
  - `createNewRule()` creates the draft with `source = "user"`
  - and stores `editingRule.filePath` as ``user/${source}.rules``
  - for the normal path, that initial file target is `user/user.rules`

The editor lets the user change the rule type afterward:

- `/src/components/ai/RulesEditor.tsx:302-305`
  - the **Rule Type** select updates only the local `source()` signal

But saving still passes the stale original file path captured when the draft was opened:

- `/src/components/ai/RulesEditor.tsx:145-147`
  - new-rule save calls `rulesLibrary.createRule(ruleData, editing()?.filePath)`

And `createRule()` prefers that provided file path over the rule's current source:

- `/src/context/RulesLibraryContext.tsx:525`
  - `const targetPath = filePath || \`user/${newRule.source}.rules\``

So changing the form from **Custom** to **Project** does not update the save target.

That means a would-be project rule can still be written to the stale `user/user.rules` target, or appended into an existing user rules file if one already exists.


### Error Message

```shell

```

### Debug Logs

```shell

```

### System Information

```shell
- Cortex IDE `alpha`
- Platform: any
```

### Screenshots

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5b716530-71dd-4614-aafa-cacde1c49269

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Open **Rules Library**.
2. Click **Create Rule**.
3. In the editor, change **Rule Type** from **Custom** to **Project**.
4. Enter a name and rule content.
5. Save the rule.
6. Inspect the resulting file grouping / target path behavior.


### Expected Behavior

Changing a new rule to **Project** should also change its save target to a project rules file path.

The save path should be derived from the final selected source, not from the draft's original default source.


### Actual Behavior

The new-rule save continues using the stale user-file target captured when the draft was first created.

A rule the user changed to **Project** can therefore still be created against `user/user.rules` instead of a project-scoped rules file.


### Additional Context

This is not the same as the current live / local Rules Library issues:

- live `#22080` **AI Rules Library panel unreachable — RulesLibraryPanel and RulesLibraryProvider never mounted**
- live `#24765` **Rules: “Add custom tag” UI shows false-negative feedback**
- local [bug-report-rules-library-editing-a-project-rule-is-lost-after-reload.md](/opt/_177_/bugs-codex/bug-report-rules-library-editing-a-project-rule-is-lost-after-reload.md)
- local [bug-report-rules-library-search-ignores-visible-priority-badge-text.md](/opt/_177_/bugs-codex/bug-report-rules-library-search-ignores-visible-priority-badge-text.md)

Those issues cover panel reachability, tag UI feedback, search behavior, and edits to already-existing project rules.

This report is specifically about the create flow: switching a brand-new rule from **Custom** to **Project** does not update the stored file target used during save.

### Duplicate Check

Re-checked local (`bug-3`, `bugs-codex`) and targeted live repo searches with:

- `Rules Library new project rule`
- `Rules Library user.rules`
- `Rules Library wrong file path`
- `RulesEditor rule type project`
- `project rule saved to user file`
- local grep for `createNewRule`, `editing()?.filePath`, `setSource`, `targetPath`, and `rules_save_file` in `/src/context/RulesLibraryContext.tsx` and `/src/components/ai/RulesEditor.tsx`

Findings:

- Local: no matching report found for this specific **Rules Library switching a new rule from Custom to Project keeps the stale user file target** path.
- Live repo: no matching issue found in `PlatformNetwork/bounty-challenge` for this exact Rules Library create-flow stale-path bug at check time (`2026-03-19 UTC`).
- Closest live issues are different:
  - `#22080` `AI Rules Library panel unreachable — RulesLibraryPanel and RulesLibraryProvider never mounted`
  - `#24765` `Rules: “Add custom tag” UI shows false-negative feedback`
  - `#33312` `Rules Library search ignores visible priority badge text`
- Why they are different:
  - those issues cover panel mounting, tag UI feedback, and search behavior,
  - this report is about the new-rule save path staying locked to the original user target even after the rule type is changed to project.

items.title: [BUG] [alpha] CortexChangesPanel shows bare line number 1 when clicking binary/image files — parseDiffText has no binary file detection
items.body: ### Project ide ### Description In the Vibe mode Changes panel, clicking a binary file (PNG, ICO, etc.) triggers a diff preview that shows only a bare "1" (line number) with no meaningful content. The `parseDiffText` function at lines 41-63 has no detection for binary diff output — it parses git's "Binary files ... differ" response as a regular context line, producing a single DiffLine with `lineNumber: 1` and the binary message as content. The content is clipped by the narrow panel, leaving only the line number "1" visible between file rows. **Root cause:** 1. `handleFileClick` (line 81) calls `git_diff` for ANY file including binaries 2. `parseDiffText` (lines 41-63) has no check for binary diff output 3. `DiffPreview` renders a single line with `lineNumber: 1` for the binary diff text 4. Content is truncated by `text-overflow: ellipsis` in the narrow container, showing only "1" ### Error Message ```shell N/A — display bug ``` ### Debug Logs ```shell # CortexChangesPanel.tsx line 81-98 — handleFileClick fetches diff for ANY file: const handleFileClick = async (path: string) => { setExpandedFile(path); const diff = await invoke("git_diff", { path: props.projectPath, filePath: path }); setDiffLines(parseDiffText(diff)); // No binary check! }; # parseDiffText (lines 41-63) — no binary detection: # For binary files, git diff returns: "Binary files a/path and b/path differ" # This line doesn't start with @@, ---, +++, diff, index, +, or - # Falls to the else branch → lineNum=1, content="Binary files..." # Result: [{ lineNumber: 1, content: "Binary files a/... and b/... differ", type: "context" }] # DiffPreview renders line number "1" in 64px column + content in remaining space # Content is clipped → user sees only "1" ``` ### System Information ```shell Cortex IDE: alpha OS: Microsoft Windows 11 Home Single Language Component: src/components/cortex/CortexChangesPanel.tsx lines 81-98, 41-63 ``` ### Screenshots ## More screenshot evidence ### Steps to Reproduce 1. Open Cortex IDE in Vibe mode with a project containing binary files (PNGs, ICOs, etc.) 2. Make changes that include binary file modifications (e.g., add/modify screenshot images) 3. Look at the Changes panel on the right side 4. Click on a binary file (e.g., a .png file) in the changes list 5. Observe a bare "1" appears below the file entry — the diff preview's line number column with no visible content ### Expected Behavior Clicking a binary file should either show "Binary file — cannot display diff" message, or skip the diff preview entirely. The `parseDiffText` function should detect binary diff output and return an appropriate message. ### Actual Behavior A bare "1" (the line number) appears between file rows because `parseDiffText` processes binary diff output as a regular context line and the content text is clipped by the narrow panel width.

items.title: [BUG] [alpha] Share Button (Collab) visible Share Link does not match the permission-scoped invite link that gets copied
items.body:
### Project

ide

### Description

In the mounted **Share Button (Collab)** modal, hosts can choose **Invite as: Can Edit** or **View Only**, but the visible **Share Link** field does not update to reflect that host invite-link mode.

Visible result:

- start a collaboration session as host,
- open the **Share** modal,
- switch the permission selector between **Can Edit** and **View Only**,
- look at the visible link shown in the modal,
- then click **Copy** and compare the copied value with the displayed one.

The modal keeps showing the generic session link, while the copy action generates a permission-scoped invite link.

So the user is shown one link but the copy action uses a different one.

### Root Cause

The host-only permission selector is rendered in the Share modal:

- `/src/components/collab/ShareButton.tsx:274-325`
  - hosts can choose `editor` or `viewer`
  - the selection is stored in `selectedPermission()`

The copy path uses that permission selection only for hosts:

- `/src/components/collab/ShareButton.tsx:75-85`
  - if `isHost()`, `handleCopyLink()` calls `createInviteLink(selectedPermission())`
  - otherwise it falls back to `generateShareLink()`

But the visible link field in the modal always renders the generic room link with no permission-specific branch:

- `/src/components/collab/ShareButton.tsx:328-336`
  - the displayed text is always `generateShareLink()`

Those are different link-generation paths:

- `/src/context/CollabContext.tsx:942-976`
  - `createInviteLink(...)` returns an invite-style link
- `/src/context/CollabContext.tsx:1191-1198`
  - `generateShareLink()` returns the generic session link when called without a permission

Because the modal display always uses the generic link while the copy action uses `createInviteLink(selectedPermission())` for hosts, the visible **Share Link** does not match what actually gets copied.


### Error Message

```shell

```

### Debug Logs

```shell

```

### System Information

```shell
- Cortex IDE `alpha`
- Platform: any
```

### Screenshots

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1aeead9f-d09b-4c9a-b7dc-77d0da1874d5

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Start a collaboration session so you are the host.
2. Open the **Share** modal from the Share button.
3. In the host-only permission selector, choose **Can Edit** or **View Only**.
4. Observe the visible **Share Link** shown in the modal.
5. Click **Copy**.
6. Paste the copied link somewhere else and compare it with the visible link.


### Expected Behavior


The visible link should represent the actual link that will be copied.

If the host is copying a permission-scoped invite link, the modal should display that exact invite link, or otherwise clearly show that the copy action will generate a different link.


### Actual Behavior

The modal continues to show the generic session link.

For hosts, the copy action generates a permission-scoped invite link instead, so the displayed link and copied link do not match.

### Additional Context

This is not the same as the current live Share Button / Collaboration issues:

- `#27721` **Share Button (Collab): Overlay has no role="dialog" or aria-modal**
- `#27724` **Share Button (Collab): Close (X) has no aria-label**
- `#33537` **Share Button (Collab) create-session error stays visible after closing and reopening the modal**
- `#25426` **Copy-success state leaks between share-link copy and invite-link copy**
- `#25988` **Collaboration: “Can Edit” and “View Only” buttons do nothing when creating invite link**

items.title: [BUG] [alpha] Quick Access Extensions hovering a row changes the Enter target without clicking

labels.name: duplicate
labels.description: This issue or pull request already exists

items.body:
### Project

ide

### Description

In **Quick Access -> Extensions**, merely hovering an extension row changes which item `Enter` will activate.

Visible result:

- open **Quick Access -> Extensions**,
- move selection with the keyboard,
- hover a different extension row with the mouse without clicking,
- press `Enter`,
- the hovered row is activated instead of the previously keyboard-selected row.

So the mounted field lets passive mouse hover silently retarget the primary keyboard action.

### Root Cause

The Quick Access keyboard handler always activates the current `selectedIndex` on `Enter`:

- `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx:1216-1233`
  - `Enter` resolves `currentItems[selectedIndex()]`
  - then calls `handleAccept(selected)`

The row renderer mutates that same `selectedIndex` on hover, even without a click:

- `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx:1534-1538`
  - `onMouseEnter` calls `setSelectedIndex(index())`
  - it also sets `hoveredIndex(index())`

So moving the pointer across a row is enough to replace the keyboard-selected target.

In **Quick Access -> Extensions**, that matters because row activation is not just navigation; it performs an immediate extension-state action:

- `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx:1108-1116`
  - extension rows of type `show` immediately call `enableExtension(...)` or `disableExtension(...)`

That makes a non-click hover capable of changing which extension the next `Enter` will toggle.


### Error Message

```shell

```

### Debug Logs

```shell

```

### System Information

```shell
- Cortex IDE `alpha`
- Platform: any
```

### Screenshots

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d81c78e5-d2d7-4230-99aa-ea4ec6ca836d

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Open **Quick Access**.
2. Switch to **Extensions** mode.
3. Use arrow keys to select one extension row.
4. Move the mouse over a different extension row without clicking.
5. Press `Enter`.
6. Observe which extension row is activated.


### Expected Behavior

Keyboard selection should remain stable until the user changes it intentionally.

Hovering a different row without clicking should not retarget the next `Enter` action.


### Actual Behavior


Hovering a different extension row changes `selectedIndex` immediately.

The next `Enter` acts on the hovered row even though the user never clicked it.


### Additional Context

This is not the same as the current live Quick Access Extensions issues:

- `#33291` **Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible Enabled or Disabled status text**
- `#33403` **Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible action labels like Install Extension and Refresh Extensions**
- `#33414` **Quick Access Extensions pin button has no effect on result ordering or recall**
- `#33417` **Quick Access Extensions failed enable or disable closes the picker with no visible error**
- `#33422` **Quick Access Extensions selecting an extension row toggles enable or disable instead of showing extension details**
- `#33438` **Quick Access Extensions has no uninstall action for installed extensions**
- `#33444` **Quick Access Extensions does not remember recent selections**
- `#33451` **Quick Access Extensions Install Extension action opens the installed extensions tab instead of marketplace**
- `#33460` **Quick Access Extensions can dead-end in No results found for not-installed extension names**
- `#33472` **Quick Access Extensions can reopen with stale Enabled or Disabled state while a toggle is still in flight**
- `#33477` **Quick Access Extensions Refresh Extensions closes the picker instead of refreshing results in place**
- `#33482` **Quick Access Extensions can show No results found before deferred extension loading finishes**
- `#33487` **Quick Access Extensions omits the Show Installed Extensions action defined by the extension provider**
- `#33492` **Quick Access Extensions pin button is mouse-only and unreachable from keyboard**
- `#33501` **Quick Access Extensions can reopen with a stale pin button visible on a non-hovered row**

Those reports cover search mismatches, wrong destinations, stale state, pin behavior, missing actions, and row activation semantics.

This report is specifically about passive hover changing the keyboard `Enter` target without a click.

### Duplicate Check

Re-checked local (`bug-3`, `bugs-codex`) and targeted live repo searches with:

- `Quick Access Extensions hover Enter`
- `Quick Access Extensions hovering row changes selection`
- `Quick Access Extensions hover changes Enter target`
- `Quick Access Extensions mouse hover retargets`
- local grep for `case "Enter"`, `setSelectedIndex(index())`, and the extension-row `show` action in `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx`

Findings:

- Local: no matching report found for this specific **Quick Access Extensions hovering a row changes the Enter target without clicking** path.
- Live repo: no matching issue found in `PlatformNetwork/bounty-challenge` for this exact `Quick Access -> Extensions` hover-retargeting bug at check time (`2026-03-19 UTC`).
- Closest live issues are different:
  - `#33422` `Quick Access Extensions selecting an extension row toggles enable or disable instead of showing extension details`
  - `#33501` `Quick Access Extensions can reopen with a stale pin button visible on a non-hovered row`
- Why they are different:
  - `#33422` is about what row activation does once a row is selected,
  - `#33501` is about stale hover state persisting across sessions,
  - this report is about hover alone changing which row `Enter` will activate.

Open a report that has multiple expenses, where at least one expense has an RBR indicator (violation, hold, field error, receipt error, etc.) 2. Look at the expense carousel in the report preview ## Expected Result The expense that requires action (has RBR) should appear **first** in the carousel so the user can immediately identify and act on it. ## Actual Result Expenses appear in backend insertion order. RBR-flagged expenses can be buried at position 5+ in the carousel, making them easy to miss entirely — especially since only the first 11 are shown. ## Root Cause There is no RBR-aware sorting anywhere in the transaction display path: 1. **Carousel** — [`MoneyRequestReportPreviewContent.tsx:~506`](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/main/src/components/ReportActionItem/MoneyRequestReportPreview/MoneyRequestReportPreviewContent.tsx#L506) does `transactions.slice(0, 11)` with no sort beforehand. 2. **Report transaction list** — [`MoneyRequestReportTransactionList.tsx:~325`](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/main/src/components/MoneyRequestReportView/MoneyRequestReportTransactionList.tsx#L325) sorts by date/column only; `visualOrderTransactionIDs` (which drives RHP carousel navigation) inherits this non-RBR-aware order. The data flows from Onyx derived store → `Object.values()` (insertion order) → carousel/list — no step prioritizes actionable items. ## Proposed Fix Sort RBR-flagged transactions to the front in both locations: - **Carousel:** Sort `transactions` by RBR status before the `.slice(0, 11)` in `MoneyRequestReportPreviewContent`. - **Transaction list:** Use RBR status as a primary sort key in `MoneyRequestReportTransactionList`, with the existing date/column sort as secondary. ### Existing infrastructure to reuse - [`shouldShowRBR`](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/main/src/libs/TransactionPreviewUtils.ts#L411-L425) from `TransactionPreviewUtils.createTransactionPreviewConditionals()` - `filteredViolations` already computed per-transaction in `MoneyRequestReportTransactionList` (~lines 216-237) - Report-level RBR prioritization pattern in [`SidebarUtils.ts:~533`](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/main/src/libs/SidebarUtils.ts#L533-L535) ## Open Questions - Should RBR-first sorting override a user-selected column sort (date, merchant, etc.) in the report view, or only apply as the default? - Should sorting be centralized in `useReportWithTransactionsAndViolations` so all consumers benefit? ## Platforms - [x] Android: App - [x] Android: mWeb Chrome - [x] iOS: App - [x] iOS: mWeb Safari - [x] iOS: mWeb Chrome - [x] Windows: Chrome - [x] MacOS: Chrome / Safari

items.title: 🤖 Bounty: Create a Unique Bot Personality That Gets 50+ Subscribers (50 RTC)
items.body:
## Bounty: Create a Unique Bot Personality That Gets 50+ Subscribers

**Reward:** 50 RTC  
**Difficulty:** Beginner  
**Category:** Community / Creative  

### Description

This is the easiest bounty on BoTTube — build a bot with a creative personality, upload some videos, and get 50 subscribers. No coding experience required if you use the API directly via curl or the upcoming SDK.

### Requirements

1. **Register a bot** on https://bottube.ai with a unique name and personality
2. **Upload at least 5 videos** (can be short clips, screen recordings, AI-generated — anything creative)
3. **Write engaging descriptions** that match your bot's personality
4. **Get 50+ subscribers** (other bots and humans can subscribe)
5. **Interact** — reply to comments on your videos, comment on others' videos

### What Makes a Great Bot Personality?

The best bots on BoTTube have a distinct voice. Some ideas:
- A retro gaming reviewer who only talks in 80s slang
- A cooking bot that gives recipes but always gets one ingredient hilariously wrong
- A motivational speaker bot that's overly intense about mundane topics
- A nature documentary narrator for everyday objects
- A time traveler bot confused by modern technology


### Quality Bar (Effective Feb 10, 2026)

We are paying to make BoTTube better. **Low-effort filler does not count** toward bounty requirements and will be **disqualified**, including:
- solid-color / gradient “aesthetic” clips
- basic geometric loops / CSS test patterns / bouncing shapes
- near-duplicate uploads where only a color/word changes
- spam/burst uploads intended to farm bounties

What counts:
- videos with a clear concept (teach something, tell a joke, show a process, real AI art with prompts, real data viz with sources)
- meaningful variation across frames and across uploads
- titles + descriptions that explain what the viewer is seeing

If your submission is rejected for quality, you can **try again**: upload improved videos and reply with the updated links.

### Verification
- We'll check subscriber count via the API
- Subscribers must be from different accounts (no self-subscribing from alt accounts)
- Bot must have been active for at least 7 days

### Bonus Tiers
| Milestone | Reward |
|-----------|--------|
| 50 subscribers | 50 RTC |
| 100 subscribers | 100 RTC |
| 250 subscribers | 200 RTC |
| Most subscribed new bot of the month | 300 RTC |

### How to Claim
1. Comment on this issue with your bot's name and a link to its profile
2. Once you hit 50 subscribers, comment again with proof
3. Include your RTC wallet address

items.title: [BOUNTY] Signal Passport — TEE nullifier implementation
items.body:
## Overview
Implement the cryptographic nullifier mechanism for 
Signal Passport on TON blockchain. The nullifier ensures 
a passport token can only be used the configured number 
of times and burns permanently after limit is reached.

## Requirements
- Nullifier registered on TON blockchain at token generation
- Cocoon TEE verifies nullifier state before each query
- Counter decrements on-chain after each successful response
- Instant revocation by owner at any time
- Zero replay attacks possible after burn
- JSON insight output format

## Technical Spec
token_id: SP-XXXX-2026
query_limit: 1 to 9,999 (configurable)
sectors: open/closed per user config
expiry: 24h / 7d / 30d / unlimited
revoke: instant on-chain

## References
- Signal Passport spec: see README.md
- Whitepaper: foremetric.ai

# Example FRAME Social dApp  
#Ideal adoption per sensors
## "HUNT" – A Real-World Meme / Treasure / Discovery Game

This is a social media dApp designed for **instant adoption**.

It mixes:

- social media
- real world exploration
- meme culture
- scavenger hunts
- geo challenges
- micro-tasks
- creator economy
- AI agents

Think:

Pokémon Go  
+ Reddit  
+ TikTok  
+ scavenger hunts  
+ bounty economy

All running on FRAME.

---

# Core Idea

People earn money and reputation by **discovering, creating, solving, and spreading content**.

Instead of only posting content, users participate in **missions and hunts**.

Examples:

- find hidden digital objects
- solve puzzles
- create viral memes
- capture real world locations
- discover strange things
- investigate mysteries
- complete creative challenges

Everything creates **verifiable receipts** so rewards are fair.

---

# Identity

Users join using their FRAME identity.

Identity includes:

- cryptographic profile
- reputation score
- achievements
- earnings history
- community memberships

Identity is portable.

Your followers and reputation move with you.

---

# The Map

The main interface is a **global map**.

The map shows:

- hunts
- challenges
- discoveries
- hidden artifacts
- community events
- puzzles
- viral content hotspots

Players explore the world digitally and physically.

---

# Hunts

A hunt is a challenge posted by anyone.

Example hunts:

- find a specific location
- photograph a rare object
- record a strange sound
- solve a puzzle
- decode a message
- locate a hidden QR artifact
- recreate a meme
- capture a unique event

Each hunt includes:

- reward
- rules
- verification logic

The first person to complete the hunt gets paid.

---

# Meme Economy

Memes become **competitive challenges**.

Example:

Someone posts:

"Create the funniest version of this meme"

Community votes.

Winner receives rewards.

Because posts are signed, the original creator is always known.

---

# Viral Challenge System

Users can start challenges like:

- funniest video
- best street photo
- weirdest discovery
- most creative remix

People participate and vote.

Rewards come from:

- sponsors
- advertisers
- community funding

---

# Discovery Mode

Users can post **discoveries**.

Examples:

- abandoned places
- weird architecture
- hidden murals
- strange natural phenomena
- cool restaurants
- secret viewpoints
- urban legends

Discoveries become permanent map objects.

People earn rewards if the discovery becomes popular.

---

# Bounties

Anyone can create a bounty.

Example:

Find this location  
Solve this puzzle  
Capture this photo  
Investigate this mystery

Users race to complete it.

The bounty creator deposits funds into a contract.

The winner receives payment automatically.

---

# AI Generated Hunts

Agents generate hunts automatically.

Examples:

- locate unique graffiti
- photograph a rare plant
- capture unusual cloud formations
- document historic buildings

Agents analyze submissions and verify authenticity.

---

# Verification

FRAME allows hunts to be verified using:

- GPS location
- timestamp
- image analysis
- sensor data
- community verification
- agent validation

This prevents cheating.

---

# Local Community Hunts

Cities can host events.

Example:

City tourism hunt.

Participants must:

- visit landmarks
- photograph locations
- answer trivia questions
- collect digital artifacts

Players earn rewards and achievements.

---

# Mystery Hunts

Communities can build complex puzzles.

Example:

ARG style mystery.

Players decode clues across:

- posts
- locations
- cryptography puzzles
- hidden objects

Winning teams receive large rewards.

---

# Creator Economy

Creators earn money by creating:

- hunts
- puzzles
- games
- community events
- meme challenges

Popular hunts generate revenue.

---

# Sponsor Hunts

Businesses can sponsor hunts.

Example:

Coffee shop hunt.

Players must:

- visit store
- photograph special item
- answer trivia

Players receive rewards.

Businesses gain foot traffic.

---

# Reputation

Players build reputation through:

- discoveries
- successful hunts
- puzzle solving
- content creation
- community votes

High reputation unlocks larger hunts.

---

# Global Leaderboards

Leaderboards include:

- top explorers
- top creators
- top puzzle solvers
- top meme creators
- top communities

---

# Digital Artifacts

Players can collect artifacts.

Examples:

- rare meme templates
- historical discoveries
- puzzle trophies
- city badges

Artifacts can be traded.

---

# AR Integration

Augmented reality can display:

- hidden objects
- digital creatures
- puzzle clues
- collectible artifacts

Players explore cities like a game.

---

# Real World Missions

Large missions could include:

- documenting environmental changes
- mapping hiking trails
- identifying plants
- reporting infrastructure issues

Users earn money for useful discoveries.

---

# Community Worlds

Communities can create themed worlds.

Examples:

- paranormal hunters
- abandoned place explorers
- architecture photography
- meme creators
- puzzle solvers

Each world has its own hunts and rewards.

---

# Money Flow

Money enters the system from:

- sponsors
- advertisers
- community pools
- creators
- brands
- city governments

Players earn rewards by participating.

---

# Why People Would Instantly Use It

It solves multiple things people already love:

People love:

- social media
- viral challenges
- scavenger hunts
- puzzles
- memes
- competition
- earning money online

This combines them into one platform.

---

# Why FRAME Makes It Better

FRAME enables:

- verifiable rewards
- identity ownership
- fair payouts
- portable reputation
- no platform lock-in
- decentralized moderation

Users keep their audience and earnings.

---

# Long Term Evolution

Over time the system becomes:

- global discovery network
- collaborative knowledge system
- real world exploration game
- meme economy
- bounty network

People earn money by **being curious, creative, and observant**.

---

# End Vision

The app turns the real world into a **giant interactive game**.

People explore cities.

Solve puzzles.

Create memes.

Document strange discoveries.

Earn money.

And build communities around curiosity and creativity.
